questions.prompt: The half-power frequencies are where the power drops to:
questions.explanation: Half-power frequencies: P(ω₁) = P(ω₂) = Pmax/2. The current drops to Imax/√2 (since P ∝ I²).

questions.prompt: The bandwidth of a series RLC circuit is:
questions.explanation: B = ω₂ − ω₁ = R/L rad/s. Alternatively, B = ω₀/Q.

questions.prompt: The relationship between Q and bandwidth is:
questions.explanation: Q = ω₀/B = centre frequency / bandwidth. Higher Q → narrower bandwidth → sharper resonance.

questions.prompt: ω₀ is related to ω₁ and ω₂ by:
questions.explanation: The resonant frequency is the geometric mean of the half-power frequencies: ω₀² = ω₁·ω₂. This is exact, not an approximation.

questions.prompt: Increasing R in a series RLC circuit causes bandwidth to:
questions.explanation: B = R/L. Increasing R increases bandwidth, making the resonance less sharp (lower Q).

questions.prompt: At the half-power frequencies, the impedance magnitude equals:
questions.explanation: At half-power: P = Pmax/2 → I = Imax/√2 → |Z| = Vm/I = Vm/(Imax/√2) = √2·R.

questions.prompt: The bandpass filter transfer function has unity gain at:
questions.explanation: At resonance, the transfer function Gv = VR/V₁ = 1 (all source voltage drops across R). It rolls off on both sides.

questions.prompt: For a high-Q circuit (Q >> 1), the half-power frequencies are approximately:
options: ω₁ ≈ ω₀ − B/2, ω₂ ≈ ω₀ + B/2
questions.correctAnswer: ω₁ ≈ ω₀ − B/2, ω₂ ≈ ω₀ + B/2
questions.explanation: For high Q, the response is nearly symmetric around ω₀, so the half-power frequencies are approximately B/2 above and below ω₀.

questions.prompt: A circuit with ω₀ = 10,000 rad/s and Q = 50 has bandwidth:
What determines the 'selectivity' of a resonant circuit?
options: The quality factor Q (or equivalently, bandwidth B)
questions.correctAnswer: The quality factor Q (or equivalently, bandwidth B)
questions.explanation: Selectivity = how sharply the circuit discriminates between frequencies. High Q → narrow B → high selectivity. Low Q → wide B → low selectivity.
